question.explanation: A strong answer names a specific role or industry context, connects the lesson content directly to a real task or decision, and shows understanding of why this knowledge matters in practice.

topics.title: Project Management
topics.description: Planning, documenting, and evaluating a digital solution project.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.

blocks.heading: 💼 Career Connection: IT Project Manager
blocks.text: Project management is one of the most in-demand IT skills. Roles like Junior Project Manager or Business Analyst rely heavily on design process thinking, time management, and client communication.
Planning a Digital Solution

The planning phase is the most important part of any digital project. Good planning prevents wasted effort, missed deadlines, and poor quality outcomes. SCSA AIT projects require structured planning documentation.
Understanding the Brief

Before planning, you must fully understand the project brief. A brief defines: the problem to be solved, the target audience, the required deliverables, the constraints (time, tools, budget), and the criteria by which the solution will be evaluated.

blocks.definition: A document that defines the purpose, scope, audience, constraints, and success criteria for a project.

blocks.text: Identifying Requirements

Requirements are what the solution must do (functional requirements) and the standards it must meet (non-functional requirements). For example: "The website must allow users to submit a contact form" is functional; "The website must load in under 3 seconds" is non-functional.

blocks.definition: What the solution must DO — features and capabilities.
Standards the solution must MEET — performance, usability, reliability, security.

blocks.text: Project Planning Tools

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Most IT employers rate communication skills as highly as technical skills. Practice explaining complex concepts in simple terms.

rows: Tasks plotted on a timeline with start/end dates and dependencies
Hierarchical breakdown of all tasks needed to complete the project
Initial planning; identifying all required work
Tasks in columns: To Do → In Progress → Done
Visual sequence of screens or scenes
Planning software logic or user journeys

blocks.definition: A horizontal bar chart that shows project tasks along a timeline, including start/end dates, duration, and dependencies between tasks.

blocks.text: Documentation

Good documentation captures your planning decisions and justifies your choices. Required planning documents in SCSA AIT projects typically include: the interpreted brief, design sketches or wireframes, a Gantt chart or schedule, and a list of required resources.
Risk Management

Identify risks early: What could go wrong? How likely is it? What would the impact be? And what will you do if it happens? Common project risks include: scope creep (project growing beyond original plan), technical problems, time underestimation, and loss of work due to not saving/backing up.

Always back up your work to a second location (e.g., USB drive AND cloud storage). A common reason for incomplete AIT projects is "my computer crashed and I lost everything" — preventable with proper backup habits.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.
Evaluating a Digital Solution

Evaluation is the process of assessing how well a completed solution meets the original brief and success criteria. Good evaluation is honest, structured, and evidence-based — not just "I think it looks good."
What to Evaluate

items: Does the solution meet all functional requirements from the brief?
Does it meet non-functional requirements (performance, usability, accessibility)?
How well does it serve the target audience?
Were design principles applied effectively?
What would be improved if you had more time?

blocks.text: Evaluation Criteria

Evaluation criteria should be defined in the planning phase — they are the measurable standards your solution will be judged against. Examples: "The interface can be navigated without instructions by a Year 10 student", "All text meets WCAG contrast requirements", "The spreadsheet produces correct totals when data is entered".

blocks.definition: Specific, measurable statements that describe what a successful solution looks like. Defined at the planning stage.

blocks.text: Methods of Evaluation

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Version control is standard practice in professional IT teams. Never overwrite a file without saving the previous version first.

rows: Compare the solution to each planned criterion — met, partially met, or not met
Have someone from the target audience use the solution and observe them
Reveals usability issues you can't see yourself
Have a classmate evaluate the solution against the criteria
Gets an outside perspective; good for finding blind spots
Honest personal assessment of strengths and weaknesses
Demonstrates critical thinking; required in SCSA tasks

blocks.text: Writing an Evaluation

A strong evaluation: (1) references specific criteria, (2) provides evidence (screenshots, data, user feedback), (3) makes a clear judgement (met / partially met / not met), and (4) suggests realistic improvements.

SCSA examiners reward honest evaluation. Saying "the colour contrast does not meet accessibility standards and I would fix this by..." is better than claiming everything is perfect. Critical thinking scores marks; defensiveness does not.
Reflection

Beyond evaluation of the product, SCSA assessments also ask you to reflect on your process: What went well? What would you do differently? How did you manage your time? What new skills did you develop? These reflections demonstrate growth and self-awareness.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.
Personal Digital Project Task

This is your Unit 1 project. You will plan, create, and evaluate a digital solution that demonstrates the skills you have built throughout the unit. This task mirrors the type of project work assessed in SCSA AIT General.
This is your major assessment task. Allow 3–4 hours across multiple sessions. Submit all documents and files as directed by your teacher.
The Brief

Your school has asked you to create an information resource for Year 9 students who are considering taking AIT General in Year 11. The resource should help them understand what the subject involves, what skills they will develop, and how digital technology is used in everyday life.
Requirements

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: When troubleshooting, always start with the simplest explanation first (cable unplugged? power on?) before assuming a complex problem.

items: The resource must be a digital document (Word/Google Docs) of at least 3 pages OR a multipage presentation (PowerPoint/Google Slides) of at least 8 slides
Target audience: Year 9 students — design and language choices must suit this audience
Must include information on: what AIT General covers, digital skills students will learn, career pathways that use IT skills, and at least one example of how technology impacts everyday life
Must demonstrate at least 4 design principles with evidence
Must include at least one table, one list, and one visual element (image, chart, or icon)
All images/resources used must be cited or be royalty-free

blocks.title: Unit 1 Digital Project
blocks.software: Microsoft Word / Google Docs / PowerPoint / Google Slides
blocks.intro: Complete all four phases: Plan, Create, Review, Evaluate. Document your work at each stage.
steps: PHASE 1 — PLAN: Write a Planning Document (1 page) that includes: your interpreted brief (what you will create and why), your target audience analysis, a list of at least 5 design decisions you will make and why, a schedule showing what you will do in each work session.
PHASE 1 — PLAN: Create a rough sketch or wireframe showing the layout of your first 3 pages/slides before you start building.

PHASE 2 — CREATE: Build your information resource according to your plan. Apply design principles consistently. Format all headings using styles (not manual formatting). Include a table, a list, and at least one visual element.
PHASE 2 — CREATE: Save your work at the end of every session to both your school drive and a second location (USB or personal cloud). Name files clearly: "AIT_Project_YourName_Draft1".
PHASE 3 — REVIEW: Ask at least one classmate to review your resource using the evaluation criteria below. Record their feedback in writing.
PHASE 3 — REVIEW: Make revisions based on the feedback received. Document what you changed and why.

PHASE 4 — EVALUATE: Write an Evaluation (1–1.5 pages) addressing each success criterion below. Be specific and honest. Include evidence (screenshots where relevant) and suggest at least two improvements.

blocks.text: Evaluation Criteria

rows: A Year 9 student can understand the document without needing extra explanation
All information about AIT is factually correct and current
At least 4 principles can be identified and justified with specific examples from the resource
Font choices are appropriate and consistent; hierarchy is clear
Text contrast is sufficient; font is large enough; layout is logical
All required content areas are covered
The resource would be suitable to display in a school setting

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.

question.text: What does a Gantt chart primarily show?
options: The budget breakdown across project phases
The hierarchy of tasks needed to complete a project
Project tasks plotted on a timeline with start and end dates
The roles and responsibilities of team members
question.explanation: A Gantt chart shows project tasks as horizontal bars on a timeline, displaying start dates, end dates, duration, and dependencies between tasks. It is one of the most common project planning and tracking tools.

question.text: A requirement that states "The website must allow users to submit a contact form" is best described as a...
question.explanation: Functional requirements describe what a system must DO — its features and capabilities. "Submit a contact form" is a specific function the system must perform. Non-functional requirements describe performance standards (speed, security, accessibility).

question.text: A good evaluation of a digital solution should always conclude that the solution fully met all requirements.
question.explanation: False. Good evaluation is honest and evidence-based. Identifying where a solution partially met or did not meet criteria, and explaining what would be done differently, demonstrates critical thinking and earns marks in SCSA assessments. Always-perfect evaluations are not credible.

question.text: Which planning document shows the logical breakdown of ALL tasks needed to complete a project in a hierarchical structure?
question.explanation: A Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) hierarchically decomposes the entire project into all necessary tasks. A Gantt chart adds timeline; a storyboard visualises screens/scenes; a flowchart shows decision logic and process steps.

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Professional IT workers keep a log of every issue they encounter and how they solved it. This becomes an invaluable personal knowledge base.

question.text: A student is evaluating their completed website by asking three Year 9 students to use it while the student watches and notes any confusion. What evaluation method is this?
question.explanation: User testing involves having someone from the target audience use the solution while the creator observes. It reveals real usability issues that the creator cannot spot because they are too familiar with the product.

question.text: Evaluation criteria should be written AFTER the project is completed so they accurately reflect what was achieved.
question.explanation: False. Evaluation criteria must be defined during the PLANNING phase — they set out what success looks like before work begins. Writing criteria after completion is dishonest and defeats the purpose; it allows criteria to be fitted to the outcome rather than the outcome being judged objectively against criteria.

question.text: Why is backing up project files to two separate locations important?
options: It is a legal requirement for all school assignments
It prevents scope creep in the project
It protects against complete loss of work if one storage device fails
It allows multiple people to edit the file at the same time
question.explanation: Saving to two locations (e.g., school drive + USB, or USB + cloud) protects against data loss from hardware failure, accidental deletion, or device loss. It is a fundamental risk management strategy for any digital project.

question.text: What is "scope creep" in a project context?
options: A type of malware that targets project management software
When the project grows beyond the original plan without proper review
When team members leave a project before completion
Running out of storage space during a project
question.explanation: Scope creep occurs when a project gradually expands beyond its original plan — adding features, sections, or requirements that were not in the original brief. It is a major cause of late projects and overruns. Good planning and sticking to the brief prevents it.

question.text: Reflect on what you've learned in this lesson. Describe how the concepts covered in "Project Management Quiz" could apply in a real IT workplace scenario. Give a specific example.
question.explanation: A strong answer names a specific role or industry context, connects the lesson content directly to a real task or decision, and shows understanding of why this knowledge matters in practice.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.

blocks.heading: 💼 Career Connection: Data Analyst & Database Administrator
blocks.text: Data management skills are foundational for Data Analysts, Database Administrators, and anyone working with business intelligence. Understanding file formats, search strategies, and version control is essential.
What is Data?
Data is raw, unprocessed facts and figures. On its own, data has no meaning — it is simply a collection of values, symbols, or observations that have not yet been interpreted or given context.

blocks.definition: Raw, unprocessed facts, figures, or symbols that have no meaning on their own.

blocks.text: The word 'data' comes from the Latin word meaning 'something given'. It is the plural of 'datum' — a single piece of information.
What is Information?

Information is data that has been processed, organised, or structured to give it meaning and context. When we interpret data and understand what it represents, we turn it into information that can be used to make decisions.

blocks.definition: Data that has been processed and given meaning through context, making it useful for decision-making.

blocks.text: Data vs Information — Examples

rows: The temperature today is 28°C — warmer than average for this time of year
The class average on the test was 86.4% — an above-average result
File sizes: 1 GB, 512 MB, and 2 GB — the third file is the largest
The school bell rings at 8:45 AM — time to be in class

blocks.text: Types of Data

items: Quantitative data — numerical data that can be measured (e.g. height, temperature, test scores)
Qualitative data — descriptive data based on characteristics or categories (e.g. colour, opinion, name)
Primary data — collected firsthand by the person who needs it (e.g. a survey you conducted yourself)
Secondary data — collected by someone else and reused for a new purpose (e.g. census data, published research)

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Cloud services like Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, and Dropbox are standard in most workplaces. Knowing them well gives you an immediate edge.
A handy way to remember: data is the 'what', information is the 'what does it mean'. Data becomes information when it's given context.
Why Does This Matter in IT?

In information technology, understanding the difference between data and information is fundamental. Every system you build or use — apps, websites, databases — collects raw data, processes it, and presents it as meaningful information. Knowing the distinction helps you design better systems and evaluate the quality of the information you work with.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: Which of the following is an example of data (not information)?
options: The average rainfall in Perth is 730 mm per year
The temperature rose by 3 degrees overnight
question.explanation: The number 47 is raw data — it has no context or meaning on its own. The other options all include context that gives numbers meaning, making them information.

question.text: Information is data that has been processed and given meaning through context.
question.explanation: True. Information is always derived from data — it is data plus context, organisation, or interpretation that makes it meaningful and useful.

question.text: A student surveys 30 classmates and records their favourite subjects. What type of data is this?
question.explanation: The student collected the data themselves (primary), and favourite subjects are descriptive categories rather than numbers (qualitative).

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.
Why Accuracy Matters

Not all information is created equal. In the digital age, anyone can publish anything online — making it critical to evaluate the accuracy and reliability of sources before using them. Poor-quality information can lead to bad decisions, misinformation, and serious real-world consequences.
A 2016 Stanford study found that 82% of middle school students could not distinguish between advertising content and real news articles. Evaluating sources is a critical digital literacy skill.
Key Terms

blocks.definition: How correct and free from errors the information is.
How consistently trustworthy a source is over time — can you depend on it?
Whether the information actually measures or represents what it claims to.
A tendency to present information in a way that favours one viewpoint, often without acknowledging it.

blocks.text: Evaluating Sources — The CRAAP Test
The CRAAP Test is a framework used to evaluate the quality of information sources. Each letter stands for a criterion to consider:

rows: When was it published or last updated? Is it still relevant?
Does it relate to your topic? Is it written for an appropriate audience?
Who wrote it? What are their credentials? Is the publisher reputable?
Is it supported by evidence? Can you verify the facts elsewhere?
Why was it written? To inform, persuade, sell, or entertain?

blocks.text: Reliable vs Unreliable Sources

rows: Sites selling products related to the topic

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Backup your work. The industry rule: 3-2-1 backup — 3 copies, on 2 different media types, with 1 offsite (cloud).
Recognising Bias

Bias does not always mean the information is wrong — but it means you are only seeing part of the picture. Watch for language that is emotionally charged, sources that only present one side of an argument, or funding that could influence the conclusions presented.
Red flags: no author listed, no publication date, claims that seem too extreme, pressure to share immediately, and URLs that mimic real news sites (e.g. ABCnews.com.co).
Check Your Understanding

question.text: Which CRAAP Test criterion asks "Who wrote this and are they qualified to do so?"
question.explanation: Authority asks about the author's credentials and the publisher's reputation. Knowing who wrote something helps you assess how much to trust the information.

question.text: A website with a .gov.au domain is generally considered a more reliable source than an anonymous blog.
question.explanation: True. Government websites are maintained by official bodies and are held to standards of accuracy. Anonymous blogs have no accountability — anyone can write anything without evidence.

question.text: Match each term to its correct definition.

pairs.definition: How correct and free from errors the information is
How consistently trustworthy a source is over time
A tendency to favour one viewpoint without acknowledging it
How recent and up to date the information is

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.
How Search Engines Work

Search engines like Google work by crawling billions of web pages, indexing their content, and ranking them based on relevance to your search query. Understanding how this works helps you search more effectively and find better-quality results.
Google processes approximately 8.5 billion searches per day. The algorithm uses over 200 factors to rank results — including relevance, page authority, and user behaviour signals.
Boolean Operators

Boolean operators are keywords that tell a search engine how to combine your search terms. They come from Boolean logic — a system of true/false values used in computing. Using them makes your searches far more precise.

blocks.definition: Both terms must appear in results. Narrows the search. Example: climate AND Australia
Either term can appear. Broadens the search. Example: dog OR puppy
Excludes results containing the second term. Example: jaguar NOT car

blocks.text: Advanced Search Techniques

rows: Returns results with that exact phrase
Searches only within a specific domain
Finds files of a specific type
Fills in the blank with any word

blocks.text: Tip: Use quotation marks for exact phrases, and combine operators for precision. Example: "network security" AND Australia site:gov.au finds Australian government pages about network security.
Putting It All Together

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Security is everyone's job, not just the IT department's. Phishing attacks succeed because employees don't recognise them — understanding these threats makes you a valuable team member.

Effective searching is a skill. Start with broad terms, then refine using operators and filters. Always evaluate results critically using what you learned in the previous lesson — just because it appears at the top of Google does not mean it is the most accurate source.

items: Start with your main keywords
Use quotation marks for exact phrases
Add AND or OR to combine or expand concepts
Use NOT or the minus sign to exclude unwanted results
Use site: to restrict to trusted domains
Evaluate results using the CRAAP criteria

blocks.text: Check Your Understanding

question.text: Which Boolean operator would you use to search for pages about either "cats" or "dogs" (but not necessarily both)?
question.explanation: OR broadens a search by returning results that contain either term. It is useful when you want to include synonyms or related concepts.

question.text: A student wants to find only PDF files about network security from educational institutions. Which search would work best?
question.explanation: Combining site:edu and filetype:pdf along with an exact phrase search gives the most targeted results — educational domain + PDF format + the exact phrase.

question.text: The NOT operator (or minus sign) removes results that contain the specified word.
question.explanation: True. For example, searching "jaguar -car" removes all pages that mention the word "car", helping you find results about the animal rather than the vehicle brand.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.
What is a File Format?

A file format is a standard way that data is encoded and stored in a file. The format determines what software can open the file, how it is compressed (if at all), and what features are supported. Choosing the right format for a task is an important practical skill in IT.

blocks.definition: The letters after the dot in a file name (e.g. .pdf, .jpg) that indicate the file format.
Reducing file size by encoding data more efficiently. Can be lossless (no quality loss) or lossy (some quality sacrificed).

blocks.text: Document and Text Formats

rows: Universal — any device can open it
Sharing documents that should not be edited
Looks the same on any device, not easily edited
Text-based, compatible with any spreadsheet app
Displayed by browsers, contains markup tags

blocks.text: Use PDF when sharing a document that must look the same for everyone. Use DOCX when the recipient needs to edit the document. Use CSV for data that needs to move between systems.
Image Formats

rows: Photos and realistic images — small file size
Images requiring transparency or crisp edges (logos, screenshots)
Simple animations, icons with flat colour
Logos, icons — scales to any size without quality loss
Web images — better quality at smaller size than JPEG/PNG

blocks.text: Audio and Video Formats

items: MP3 (.mp3) — Lossy compressed audio. Small file size, standard for music and podcasts
WAV (.wav) — Uncompressed audio. High quality but large file size. Used in professional audio production
MP4 (.mp4) — Compressed video with audio. Most common video format for sharing and streaming
MOV (.mov) — Apple QuickTime format. High quality, often used for video editing
AVI (.avi) — Older Microsoft video format. Less compressed than MP4

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: In the IT industry, documentation matters as much as the technical work itself. Always write clear notes, comments, and reports — they're what clients and colleagues rely on.
Choosing the Right Format
Three questions to ask when choosing a file format: (1) Will the recipient need to edit it? (2) Does file size matter? (3) Does quality/transparency need to be preserved?
Check Your Understanding

question.text: A graphic designer creates a company logo that needs to scale to any size without losing quality. Which format is most appropriate?
question.explanation: SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) is a vector format that can scale to any size without losing quality. JPEG, GIF, and PNG are raster formats that become pixelated when enlarged.

question.text: JPEG uses lossless compression, meaning no image data is lost when the file is saved.
question.explanation: False. JPEG uses lossy compression, which discards some image data to achieve smaller file sizes. This is why JPEG images can show "artefacts" or become blurry when heavily compressed. PNG uses lossless compression.

question.text: A teacher wants to share a report that students must not be able to edit, and it must look identical on all devices. Which format should they use?
question.explanation: PDF (Portable Document Format) preserves formatting across all devices and is not easily editable. DOCX can be edited in Word, TXT loses all formatting, and CSV is for data.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.
Why Organisation Matters

Poor file organisation wastes time, causes version confusion, and can lead to losing important work. A well-structured filing system makes it easy to find files, collaborate with others, and back up your work effectively. Good habits built now will serve you throughout your career.
Folder Hierarchies

Files are stored in a hierarchical structure — folders inside folders. This mirrors how you might organise physical folders in a filing cabinet. The top-level is called the root directory, and you navigate down through folders to reach files.

blocks.definition: The top-level folder of a storage location (e.g. C:\ on Windows, / on Mac/Linux).
The address of a file or folder within the hierarchy (e.g. C:\Users\Student\Documents\AIT\Unit2\notes.docx).
A folder inside another folder.

blocks.text: Good folder structure example: School > Year 11 > AIT > Unit 2 > Managing Data > research-notes.docx. Each level narrows the location.
File Naming Conventions

items: Be descriptive — "unit2-networks-research.docx" beats "document1.docx"
Use dates for versioned files — "report-2025-05-24.docx" sorts chronologically
Avoid spaces — use hyphens (-) or underscores (_) instead (spaces cause issues in some systems)
Keep it concise — long names are hard to read in file browsers
Use consistent capitalisation — either all-lowercase or Title-Case, not random

blocks.text: Never use these characters in file names: \ / : * ? " < > |  — they are reserved by operating systems and will cause errors.
Storage Types

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Most IT employers rate communication skills as highly as technical skills. Practice explaining complex concepts in simple terms.

rows: Lost if device fails or stolen
Can be lost, damaged, or forgotten
Requires internet, privacy concerns, subscription costs

blocks.text: Follow the 3-2-1 backup rule: keep 3 copies of important files, on 2 different types of storage, with 1 copy off-site (e.g. in the cloud).
Check Your Understanding

question.text: Which file name follows best practice conventions?
question.explanation: unit2-assignment-draft-2025-05.docx is descriptive, uses hyphens instead of spaces, includes a date for version control, and is concise. The others use spaces, vague names, or are too abbreviated.

question.text: Cloud storage is always more secure than local storage because your files are backed up automatically.
question.explanation: False. While cloud storage provides automatic backups, it also introduces risks: your data is stored on someone else's servers, requires internet access, and is subject to that company's privacy policies and potential data breaches. Both storage types have pros and cons.

question.text: What backup rule recommends keeping 3 copies of files, on 2 different storage types, with 1 copy off-site?
question.explanation: The 3-2-1 backup rule is an industry best practice: 3 copies, 2 different media types, 1 off-site (e.g. cloud). This protects against device failure, theft, and physical disasters.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.

question.text: Which of the following best describes "data"?
options: Processed facts presented in a useful form
Raw, unprocessed facts and figures without context
A collection of web pages indexed by a search engine
The meaning behind a set of statistics
question.explanation: Data is raw, unprocessed facts and figures. It only becomes information when context is added to give it meaning.

question.text: A doctor receives blood test results showing a patient's cholesterol level as "6.2". What transforms this data into useful information?
options: Comparing it to normal ranges and explaining what it means for the patient's health
question.explanation: Context and interpretation are what transform raw data (the number 6.2) into information. Knowing that 6.2 mmol/L is above the recommended range gives the number meaning.

question.text: Qualitative data is always expressed as numbers.
question.explanation: False. Qualitative data is descriptive — it describes characteristics or categories (e.g. colour, opinion, name). Quantitative data is expressed as numbers.

question.text: The CRAAP Test criterion "Authority" asks you to evaluate which of the following?
options: Who wrote it and whether they are qualified
Whether the claims can be verified elsewhere
question.explanation: Authority focuses on the credentials of the author and the reputation of the publisher. It helps you determine whether the source is trustworthy.

question.text: Which of the following is generally considered the most reliable type of source?
options: A social media post by a celebrity
question.explanation: Peer-reviewed academic journals are vetted by experts in the field before publication. Anonymous blogs, social media posts, and commercial sites have little accountability.

question.text: Which Boolean operator would you use to find results about "renewable energy" but NOT about "solar"?
question.explanation: The NOT operator (or minus sign in Google) excludes results containing the specified word. Searching "renewable energy NOT solar" (or "renewable energy -solar" in Google) filters out solar-related results.

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Version control is standard practice in professional IT teams. Never overwrite a file without saving the previous version first.

question.text: A student needs to share a document that must look identical on all devices and must not be edited by the recipient. Which format is best?
question.explanation: PDF preserves formatting across all devices and is not easily editable. It is the standard format for sharing final documents.

question.text: JPEG images use lossless compression, preserving all original image data.
question.explanation: False. JPEG uses lossy compression — it removes some image data to reduce file size. This is acceptable for photographs but not ideal for images requiring crisp edges or transparency. PNG uses lossless compression.

question.text: Which of these file names follows best practice?
question.explanation: ait-unit2-assignment-2025-05.docx is descriptive, uses hyphens, includes a date, and has no spaces or ambiguous versioning.

question.text: The 3-2-1 backup rule recommends keeping how many copies of important files?
question.explanation: The 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, on 2 different storage media types, with 1 copy stored off-site (e.g. in the cloud). This protects against multiple types of data loss.

question.text: Reflect on what you've learned in this lesson. Describe how the concepts covered in "Managing Data Review Quiz" could apply in a real IT workplace scenario. Give a specific example.
question.explanation: A strong answer names a specific role or industry context, connects the lesson content directly to a real task or decision, and shows understanding of why this knowledge matters in practice.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.

blocks.heading: 💼 Career Connection: Network Engineer & IT Infrastructure
blocks.text: Network Engineers and Systems Administrators design and maintain the networks that businesses run on. Cisco certifications (CCNA) and CompTIA Network+ test these concepts at a professional level.
What is a Network?

A computer network is a collection of devices connected together to share resources and communicate. Networks allow devices to share files, printers, internet connections, and applications. Understanding network components is essential for anyone working in IT.

blocks.definition: Two or more devices connected together to share resources and communicate.
Any device connected to a network (computer, smartphone, printer, etc.).

blocks.text: Core Network Components

blocks.definition: Modulator-Demodulator. Connects your home or office to the internet via your ISP (Internet Service Provider). Converts digital signals to the format used by the ISP's network.
Directs data packets between devices on your network and to the internet. Assigns IP addresses to devices and manages traffic. Usually combined with the modem in home setups.
Broadcasts a Wi-Fi signal that devices connect to wirelessly. In homes, this is usually built into the router. In large buildings, multiple WAPs are used to provide coverage.
Hardware inside a device that allows it to connect to a network — either via cable (ethernet port) or wirelessly (Wi-Fi chip).
Connects multiple wired devices on the same network. Smarter than a hub — sends data only to the intended device, not all devices.

blocks.text: How Wi-Fi Works

Wi-Fi uses radio waves to transmit data between devices and a wireless access point. When you send a request (e.g. loading a website), your device sends radio signals to the nearest access point, which routes the request through the router to the internet and back.

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: When troubleshooting, always start with the simplest explanation first (cable unplugged? power on?) before assuming a complex problem.
2.4 GHz vs 5 GHz

rows: More congested — shared with microwaves, baby monitors
Less congested — fewer devices use it
Devices far from the router, IoT devices
Streaming, gaming, devices near the router

blocks.text: Types of Networks

rows: Bluetooth headphones connected to your phone
Computers connected at school or home
The internet; connections between bank branches

blocks.text: Check Your Understanding

question.text: Which device connects your home network to the internet via your ISP?
question.explanation: The modem (Modulator-Demodulator) connects your network to the internet through your ISP. It converts signals between your network and the ISP's infrastructure.

question.text: A student is gaming online and needs the fastest, most reliable connection. Their device is close to the router. Which Wi-Fi band should they use?
options: 5 GHz — it is faster and less congested
question.explanation: 5 GHz offers faster speeds and is less congested than 2.4 GHz. For a device close to the router where range is not an issue, 5 GHz is the better choice for gaming and streaming.

question.text: Match each network type to its correct description.

pairs.definition: Personal network covering a few metres, e.g. Bluetooth
Network covering a building or campus, e.g. school network
Network covering large distances, e.g. the internet
Device that broadcasts a Wi-Fi signal for devices to connect to

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.
Choosing the Right Hardware

Setting up or upgrading a network requires selecting the right hardware for your specific situation. The ideal choice depends on the environment (home, school, office), the number of devices, the required speed, and budget constraints.
Key Factors to Consider

items: Speed — What data transfer rate is needed? (Measured in Mbps or Gbps)
Range — How large is the space that needs coverage?
Number of devices — How many devices will connect simultaneously?
Security — Does the hardware support modern security protocols (WPA3)?
Cost — What is the budget? Consider upfront cost and ongoing costs (e.g. subscriptions)
Reliability — Is the hardware from a reputable manufacturer with good support?

blocks.text: Wired vs Wireless

rows: Faster and more consistent (up to 10 Gbps)
Can be affected by walls, other devices
Flexible — connect from anywhere in range
Less secure — signals can be intercepted
Lower setup cost, higher ongoing management
Desktop PCs, servers, smart TVs, gaming consoles

blocks.text: Router Selection Guide

rows: Dual-band router (2.4 + 5 GHz), 300+ Mbps, good range
Mesh Wi-Fi system (multiple nodes), dual-band, 600+ Mbps
Business-grade router, managed switch, multiple WAPs, Gigabit speeds

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Professional IT workers keep a log of every issue they encounter and how they solved it. This becomes an invaluable personal knowledge base.
For large spaces with dead zones, a mesh Wi-Fi system (like Google Nest or Eero) uses multiple nodes that work together to provide seamless coverage — better than a single router or range extender.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: A school wants to set up a computer lab with 25 desktop PCs that need fast, reliable connections. Which network type is most appropriate?
options: Wireless only — Wi-Fi is fast enough
Wired Ethernet — more reliable and faster
question.explanation: Wired Ethernet is the best choice for a computer lab. It provides consistent high speeds, is not affected by interference, and is more secure than wireless. Desktop PCs do not need mobility.

question.text: A mesh Wi-Fi system is a good solution for a large home with multiple floors and dead zones.
question.explanation: True. Mesh Wi-Fi systems use multiple nodes that communicate with each other to provide seamless coverage throughout large or multi-floor spaces. They automatically route traffic through the strongest node.

question.text: Which factor is MOST important when selecting network hardware for a hospital environment?
question.explanation: In critical environments like hospitals, reliability (the network must never go down) and security (patient data must be protected) are paramount. Cost and range are secondary considerations.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: A small business hasn't updated their router password or antivirus in 2 years. What risks does this create and what should they do?
Why Network Security Matters

Every device connected to a network is a potential target for attackers. Network security involves protecting the hardware, software, and data that make up a network from unauthorised access, misuse, damage, or attacks. Understanding threats and countermeasures is fundamental for anyone in IT.
Cybercrime costs the global economy over $8 trillion annually (2023). Most breaches involve human error or weak security practices — not sophisticated hacking.
Common Network Threats

blocks.definition: Malicious software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access to systems. Includes viruses, ransomware, spyware, and trojans.
Fraudulent messages (usually email) that trick users into revealing passwords, credit card details, or downloading malware.
Intercepting data transmitted over a network — particularly dangerous on unsecured public Wi-Fi.
An attacker secretly intercepts and potentially alters communications between two parties who believe they are communicating directly.
Flooding a server or network with traffic to make it unavailable to legitimate users.

blocks.text: Security Measures

rows: Monitors and filters incoming/outgoing network traffic based on rules
Scrambles data so it can only be read by authorised parties with the key
Creates an encrypted tunnel for your internet traffic, hiding your activity and IP
Latest Wi-Fi encryption protocol — stronger than WPA2
Requires a second verification step (e.g. SMS code) beyond a password
Account takeover even if password is stolen
Scans files, downloads, and programs for known malware, blocking or quarantining threats automatically
Viruses, trojans, ransomware, spyware, and other malware
Restricts which devices can connect to the network

blocks.text: Wi-Fi Security Protocols

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Cloud services like Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, and Dropbox are standard in most workplaces. Knowing them well gives you an immediate edge.
Wi-Fi security protocols encrypt the data sent between your device and the router. Using an outdated protocol is like leaving your door unlocked.

rows: Crackable in minutes with free tools
Improved over WEP but still vulnerable
Current standard in most devices; use AES encryption
Strongest current standard; much harder to brute-force

blocks.text: Strong passwords are your first line of defence. Use a password manager to generate and store unique, complex passwords for each account. Never reuse passwords.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: A student uses the school's open public Wi-Fi to log into their bank account. What is the main security risk?
options: An attacker could intercept their login credentials via eavesdropping
Their device might run out of battery
The bank might block the connection
question.explanation: Open (unsecured) public Wi-Fi does not encrypt traffic. An attacker on the same network could use packet sniffing tools to intercept unencrypted login credentials. Always use a VPN or HTTPS on public Wi-Fi.

question.text: WPA3 is a stronger and more secure Wi-Fi encryption protocol than WPA2.
question.explanation: True. WPA3, released in 2018, introduced stronger encryption and better protection against password guessing attacks (brute force and dictionary attacks). It is the recommended standard for new Wi-Fi networks.

question.text: Which security measure creates an encrypted tunnel for internet traffic, protecting users on public Wi-Fi?
question.explanation: A VPN (Virtual Private Network) encrypts all internet traffic and routes it through a secure server, hiding your activity and protecting you from eavesdropping — especially useful on public Wi-Fi.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.
Connecting a Device to a Network

Connecting devices to a network is a routine IT task. Whether wired or wireless, the process follows predictable steps. Knowing how to do this — and how to fix it when it goes wrong — is an essential practical skill.
Connecting via Wi-Fi (Step by Step)

items: Open your device's Wi-Fi settings (Settings > Wi-Fi or Network)
Ensure Wi-Fi is turned on
Wait for your device to scan for available networks (SSIDs)
Select your network from the list
Enter the Wi-Fi password (passphrase) when prompted
Wait for the device to obtain an IP address via DHCP
Test the connection — open a browser or ping a known address

blocks.definition: Service Set Identifier — the name of a Wi-Fi network as it appears in the list of available networks.
Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol — automatically assigns an IP address to a device when it connects to a network.
A unique numerical address assigned to each device on a network (e.g. 192.168.1.5). Allows devices to find and communicate with each other.

blocks.text: Common Problems and Solutions

rows: Cannot see network in Wi-Fi list
Router turned off; device too far away; 5GHz not visible on old device
Check router power; move closer; check frequency compatibility
Re-enter carefully; check router label or ask admin
Connected to Wi-Fi but no internet
ISP outage; router needs restart; DNS issue
Restart router/modem; check ISP status; try alternate DNS
Network congestion; interference; too many devices
Use 5 GHz band; move closer to router; disconnect unused devices
Two devices assigned the same IP
Restart both devices; router will reassign via DHCP

blocks.text: Diagnostic Tools

blocks.definition: A command that sends a test packet to an IP address to check if it is reachable and measure response time. Usage: ping 8.8.8.8 (Google's DNS server).
Windows command (ipconfig) or Mac/Linux command (ifconfig) that shows your device's network configuration — IP address, subnet mask, default gateway.

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Backup your work. The industry rule: 3-2-1 backup — 3 copies, on 2 different media types, with 1 offsite (cloud).

blocks.definition: Shows the path data takes from your device to a destination, helping identify where a connection is failing.

blocks.text: The "turn it off and on again" cliché works because most network issues are caused by stale states or cached data. Always try restarting the device and router before deeper troubleshooting.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: A student's laptop is connected to Wi-Fi but cannot load any websites. What should they try FIRST?
options: Switch to a different internet service provider
question.explanation: Restarting the router and modem clears temporary issues and forces the router to reconnect to the ISP. This resolves the majority of "connected but no internet" problems and should always be the first step.

question.text: What does the ping command test?
options: The speed of your Wi-Fi signal
Whether a remote device is reachable and how long it takes to respond
The strength of your wireless encryption
question.explanation: The ping command sends test packets to an IP address and measures whether they arrive and how long the round trip takes (in milliseconds). A high ping or packet loss indicates connectivity problems.

question.text: DHCP automatically assigns an IP address to a device when it connects to a network.
question.explanation: True. DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) is a service that runs on routers and servers. When your device joins a network, it requests an IP address from the DHCP server, which assigns one from its pool of available addresses.

blocks.heading: 🌐 Real World Scenario
blocks.text: In the real world, this topic comes up constantly in IT roles. As you work through this lesson, think about how it applies to the kind of IT job you'd like to do.

question.text: Which device in a home network connects to your ISP and converts signals for use on your local network?
question.explanation: The modem (Modulator-Demodulator) is the device that connects your home to your ISP. It converts signals between the ISP's network and your local network. The router then manages traffic within your home.

question.text: What does NIC stand for?
question.explanation: NIC stands for Network Interface Card — the hardware component inside a device that enables it to connect to a network, either via Ethernet cable or Wi-Fi.

question.text: 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi has greater range than 5 GHz Wi-Fi.
question.explanation: True. 2.4 GHz radio waves have a longer wavelength that passes through walls and obstacles better than 5 GHz. However, 5 GHz is faster and less congested where range is not a concern.

question.text: A school connects all computers in a single building into a shared network. What type of network is this?
question.explanation: A LAN (Local Area Network) covers a limited area such as a building or campus. It is the most common type of network in schools, homes, and businesses.

question.text: Which Wi-Fi security protocol is currently the strongest and most recommended?
question.explanation: WPA3 (released 2018) is the current strongest Wi-Fi security protocol. WEP is broken, WPA is weak, and WPA2 is acceptable but being superseded by WPA3.

question.text: What does a firewall primarily do?
options: Monitors and filters network traffic based on security rules
question.explanation: A firewall monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predefined security rules. It blocks unauthorised access while allowing legitimate traffic through.

blocks.heading: 🏭 Industry Tip
blocks.text: Security is everyone's job, not just the IT department's. Phishing attacks succeed because employees don't recognise them — understanding these threats makes you a valuable team member.

question.text: A VPN encrypts your internet traffic and can protect you when using public Wi-Fi.
question.explanation: True. A VPN (Virtual Private Network) creates an encrypted tunnel for all your internet traffic. This prevents eavesdroppers on public Wi-Fi from intercepting your data.

question.text: What command would you use to test whether a remote server (e.g. 8.8.8.8) is reachable from your device?
question.explanation: The ping command sends test packets to an IP address and reports whether they arrived and how long the round trip took. It is the standard first step in testing network connectivity.

question.text: A device is 'connected' to Wi-Fi but cannot access the internet. What should you try first?
question.explanation: Restarting the router and modem clears temporary faults and reconnects to the ISP. This resolves the vast majority of "connected but no internet" issues and should always be the first troubleshooting step.

question.text: Which is the BEST network connection for a desktop computer being used for video editing and file transfers in a fixed location?
question.explanation: Wired Ethernet provides the fastest and most reliable connection for fixed devices. For data-intensive tasks like video editing, a stable wired connection is always preferred over wireless.

question.text: Reflect on what you've learned in this lesson. Describe how the concepts covered in "Networks Review Quiz" could apply in a real IT workplace scenario. Give a specific example.
question.explanation: A strong answer names a specific role or industry context, connects the lesson content directly to a real task or decision, and shows understanding of why this knowledge matters in practice.
